section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT CHARTER PRICE ACTION MODEL 9: ONE SHOT ONE KILL
content:
This model, 'One Shot One Kill,' is a single approach to utilizing the mentorship information, aimed at
consistently stalking 50 to 75 pips per week.
Key expectations for developing traders:
- It is not the goal for this to happen every single week, especially for new traders.
- Be realistic with expectations and do not put too much pressure on yourself.
- Understand that losses will occur.
- Recognize that there is a 'growing pain' period before achieving a consistent model where you realize
how little you actually have to do. Do not let losses become mountainous barriers to development.

section_title: WEEKLY RANGE EXPANSION AND DIRECTIONAL BIAS
content:
Price is fractal, and the foundational stage for this model is the Weekly Range Expansion.
Our approach for building directional bias:
- We look at institutional order flow on a weekly chart to ascertain the weekly direction.
- Determine if price will expand to the upside or downside relative to the weekly institutional order flow.
- Monthly charts can also provide assistance.
- The breakdown and top-down analysis approach, as detailed in Month 12 (August 2017 content) of the
mentorship, is crucial for all analysis.
- The weekly range expansion is the consistent 'one trick pony' for directional bias. This dictates the
direction to trade for daily bias.

section_title: NAVIGATING WEEKLY BIAS AND MODEL ADHERENCE
content:
While the weekly range expansion sets the directional bias, it is important to understand:
- Not every single day (Monday through Friday) will trade in that direction; it's not necessary for it to do so.
- There will be times when the weekly prediction is incorrect, and the week may do something unexpected.
This is typical and normal.
- New traders often assume 100% accuracy, which is unrealistic.
- Sticking to the model, even when encountering adverse results, will eventually bring you back in line with
institutional order flow and smart money's dealing with price action.

section_title: SETUP AND PATTERN FOR MODEL 9: HUNTING THE WEEKLY HIGH/LOW
content:
This model focuses on specific days and patterns:
Setup:
- Focus on the day of the week: Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday.
- Expect a higher low to form within these days (for a long bias).
- Unlike Model Number 8, this model does concern itself with hunting the weekly high or low.
- This involves consistently finding what the weekly range will do and identifying sweet spot entries.
Pattern:
- Working with inefficiencies: liquidity voids and fair value gaps.
- Focusing on two types of liquidity runs.
- This lesson provides insights about liquidity not previously shared in other mentorship lessons.

section_title: HIGHER TIME FRAME FRAMEWORK AND NARRATIVE ADHERENCE
content:
The underlying framework remains unchanged, based on a higher time frame top-down analysis approach
using the weekly range expansion.
Key principles:
- We are always looking for a range between the next 50 to 100 pips.
- The higher time frame premise or directional premise is built on institutional order flow as taught in the
mentorship.
- We stick with this narrative and do not care about external market commentary (Twitter, YouTube, CNBC).
- We adhere to the price action narrative until it is proven invalid, at which point losses are cut short, and
analysis is conducted for the opposite direction.
- Working off the high time frame builds a directional bias to ensure institutional order flow on the Daily, 4
- hour, and 1-hour charts is in sync, increasing trade success likelihood.
- Since this model involves lower time frame entries and smaller moves (outside swing/position trading),
there's more potential for setups but also for wrong entries. Therefore, limit focus to Monday, Tuesday, and
Wednesday.

section_title: MENTORSHIP STUDY APPROACH AND PERSONAL TRADING STYLE DISCOVERY
content:
Engaging with the mentorship content effectively:
- The mentorship content is extensive and should be revisited every six months, cycling through the
lessons repeatedly.
- The goal is to know what will be said before it is said, signifying deep understanding of the lessons.
- The monthly lessons provide the fundamental language for building these models.
- The 12 unique models are introduced and built upon over time (e.g., 2019, 2020), adding more insights.
- Study all models broadly to discover which one you gravitate towards; this will likely be closest to your
natural trading style.
- Be flexible, as your trading style (short-term, swing, position, scalper) may evolve over time. The
mentorship helps discover what makes you 'tick' as a trader and provides tools to excel at it.

This lesson discloses advanced insights into liquidity, previously reserved for charter membership:
Two classes of liquidity taught in the mentorship:
- External Range Liquidity: Runs on outside double top highs and double bottom lows.
- Internal Range Liquidity: Fair value gaps, liquidity voids, and order blocks.
Key undisclosed insight:
- Most often, Internal Range Liquidity entries will run to External Range Liquidity.
- This means order blocks, fair value gaps, or voids serve as your entry, expanding outside the present
short-term dealing range to target external range liquidity, which is your low resistance liquidity run.
- Conversely, if employing Turtle Soup entry style (fading double top highs that are run out in a bearish
condition), your entry targets Internal Range Liquidity for exits.

This concept is presented as the 'coder key' or the fundamental framework for all trades:
Relationship between External and Internal Range Liquidity:
- All trades are framed on one of two conditions:
- Entering on External Range Liquidity (e.g., a double top sweep) and aiming for Internal Range Liquidity
(fair value gaps, equilibrium, order blocks) as targets.
- Entering on Internal Range Liquidity (e.g., a fair value gap, liquidity void fill, or order block) and aiming
for External Range Liquidity as targets for exits or scaling out.
- This approach brings consistency because it focuses on the underlying orders in the marketplace.
- When a double top (Candy Land) is run, stops are swept, and price is expected to return inside the
previous range to seek internal range liquidity, such as fair value gaps, equilibrium, or bullish order blocks,
to recapitalize at those levels.
- When price trades outside of a range, it becomes an ideal point to exit or scale out positions.

With these elements understood, a complete model is available at your fingertips to quickly ascertain
market conditions on any chart, any time frame:
Key steps:
- Identify the current trading range.
- Determine the higher time frame's most likely probable direction.
- Locate inefficiencies and imbalances.
Understanding Imbalances and Inefficiencies:
- An imbalance (e.g., buy side imbalance) and an inefficiency (e.g., sell side inefficiency) are often used
interchangeably to describe the same phenomenon.
- When the market rallies quickly from a significant low with large up candles, it creates a 'buy side
imbalance
- sell side inefficiency.' This means there weren't enough offers to pair up with the aggressive
buying, leading to 'porous' price action or gaps.
- Conversely, a 'sell side imbalance
- buy side inefficiency' occurs when the market runs down quickly,
lacking sufficient bids to balance the aggressive selling.
